articles.title: Banking App Glitch Exposes User Transactions
articles.summary: A significant glitch in the mobile apps of Lloyds, Halifax, and Bank of Scotland allowed customers to view transactions and sensitive data from other users' accounts. The issue, which occurred between 07:00 and 09:00, was tracked by Downdetector.com, showing spikes in reports. One affected customer reported seeing six different accounts on the Bank of Scotland app, including National Insurance numbers, transactions from distant locations like a pub in Newcastle, wage payments, and benefits from the Department for Work and Pensions. This exposed personal details such as employment and identity information. Lloyds Banking Group, which owns all three banks, apologized and resolved the incident, though the exact number of affected users remains unclear. This is not the first time the group has faced such problems; last year, an outage impacted 1.2 million people in the UK, with 700,000 being Lloyds customers. The glitch raises concerns about data security and the reliability of banking technology.

articles.title: The Pussycat Dolls Reunite as a Trio for New Music and Global Tour
articles.summary: The Pussycat Dolls, a renowned girl group, have announced their reunion as a trio consisting of Nicole Scherzinger, Ashley Roberts, and Kimberly Wyatt. They have released a new single titled Club Song and will embark on a 53-date world tour called the PCD Forever tour, starting in North America in June and moving to Europe in September, with nine shows in the UK and Ireland. This reunion follows a six-year delay due to the Covid pandemic and legal issues, and it coincides with Scherzinger's recent success in Sunset Boulevard. The group, famous for hits like Buttons and Don't Cha, originally formed in 2005 with six members, but Jessica Sutta, Carmit Bachar, and Melody Thornton are not participating this time. Wyatt explained that the group's lineup has always been fluid, and they feel united now to move forward. Scherzinger initiated the reunion, and the members expressed excitement about celebrating their current stage as women and returning to the stage with energetic performances.

articles.title: Investigating Fairness in England's Student Loan Repayment System
articles.summary: The Treasury Committee in England has launched an inquiry into student loans due to widespread dissatisfaction among graduates. This investigation focuses on whether recent changes, such as freezing the repayment threshold at £29,385 from 2027 to 2030 instead of adjusting it for inflation, are fair. Graduates with Plan 2 loans, issued between 2012 and 2023 in England and still in Wales, repay 9% of earnings above this threshold. The freeze means they start repaying sooner and a larger portion of their salary goes toward loans, leading to concerns about high marginal tax rates. Campaigners argue for reversing freezes, lowering repayment and interest rates, which are currently based on RPI inflation plus up to 3%. Dame Meg Hillier, chair of the committee, highlights that many graduates didn't fully understand their repayment terms and that changes might unfairly burden younger people. A case study of Natalie Whittaker shows her debt increased from £52,000 to £75,500 despite repayments, emphasizing issues with transparency. The Department for Education defends the freezes as protecting taxpayers and students, but the inquiry will assess if the system is reasonable compared to broader graduate taxation.

articles.title: U.S. Strategic Oil Release Amid Price Surge and Iran Conflict
articles.summary: The United States plans to release 172 million barrels of oil from the Strategic Petroleum Reserve as part of an international effort to address rising oil prices, which have been exacerbated by the war in Iran. Energy Secretary Chris Wright announced that the release will commence next week and span approximately 120 days, based on planned discharge rates. Additionally, the U.S. intends to replenish the reserve by adding about 200 million barrels within the next year. Currently, the SPR holds over 415 million barrels, providing a significant buffer. President Donald Trump, who previously downplayed the reserve's use, confirmed his administration would slightly reduce it and then refill it. This move follows Trump's criticism of former President Joe Biden for similar actions aimed at lowering gas prices. Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer expressed skepticism, arguing that Trump's policies have created additional chaos, such as the Strait of Hormuz blockade, and that this release may not fully resolve the underlying issues. The initiative is coordinated with the International Energy Agency to stabilize global oil markets and mitigate economic impacts on consumers.

articles.title: Trump Administration Launches New Trade Investigation to Replace Overturned Tariffs
articles.summary: The Trump administration has initiated a new trade investigation under Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974, aiming to replace tariffs that were struck down by the Supreme Court in February. This move comes after the court declared an economic emergency invalid, leading to lost revenues. The investigation targets multiple countries, including China, the European Union, and Mexico, examining factors like excess industrial capacity, government subsidies, and trade surpluses to determine if foreign companies have unfair advantages. U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer emphasized that the policy goal remains protecting American jobs, though the tools may change due to legal challenges. This process could reignite global economic tensions and impact existing trade agreements, occurring amidst midterm elections and international conflicts like the war in Iran. Additionally, a separate investigation into forced labor imports is being rolled out.

articles.title: Iran's Attacks in Strait of Hormuz Escalate Global Energy Crisis
articles.summary: In response to strikes by the United States and Israel on Iranian targets, Iran has retaliated by attacking multiple cargo ships in the Strait of Hormuz, a critical maritime chokepoint for global oil transportation. These attacks, including on a Thai navy-manned vessel, have effectively halted traffic through the strait, which connects the Persian Gulf to the world's oceans and handles about one-fifth of the world's oil supply. Iran's military spokesperson declared that no oil will be allowed to pass through to benefit the U.S., Israel, or their partners, labeling such vessels as legitimate targets. This escalation has intensified the Persian Gulf energy crunch, prompting countries worldwide to take unprecedented measures to maintain oil flow amid rising fears of supply disruptions. The situation highlights the geopolitical tensions in the region and their direct impact on global energy security, as conflicts threaten to destabilize essential trade routes.
